content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      Alice                                    Date  :  Jan. 9, 1981


From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION                A- 81-01-003
         Sarah Cameron

Subject: Computing the reporting threshold for income to a business
         entity when the filer and the filer's spouse both have a
         share of the entity.


         Under the definition of "income" in Section 82030, an
individual's income includes a pro rata share of any income of
a business entity in which the individual or spouse has a
10 percent or greater interest. In addition, a filer's income
includes a community property interest in the income of a spouse.
Since a community property interest is 50%, a filer has a 50%
interest in the spouse's pro rata share of income to a business
entity, in addition to the filer's own pro rata share. Thus,
if a filer has a 10% share and his or her spouse also has a
10% share, the filer is attributed with a 15% share for purposes
of determining which sources of income to the business entity
are reportable. This is because half of the spouse share, i.e.,
5% is added to the filer's 10% share.

         Sources of income to a business entity are reportable at
a $10,000 pro rata level. Thus in the situation described above,
the filer would have to report anyone who paid roughly $66,666.66
in gross receipts to the business entity, because 15% of $66,666.66
is $9999.99. So 15% of slightly more than that amount would be
$10,000. Thus, if the filer and the filer's spouse both share
in a business entity, the threshold remains the same, $10,000,
but the filer's pro rata share increases for determining that
the threshold has been met. You should not think of the threshold
as being lowered in such a situation, you should focus on what the
pro rata share attributable to the filer is.

         A good example of why you should not try to think in terms
of lowering the threshold is the following: the filer owns 10%
and the filer's spouse owns 50%. In this situation, it's not
correct to think of the threshold as being lowered to $6,666.66.
That only works when the shares of the filer and the spouse are
equal. In this case, the filer is attributed with his or her own
10% share and half of the spouse's share, i.e. 25%. So the filer
must report if a 35% share of the gross receipts of a client or
customer is $10,000. As I figure it out, this means someone who
has paid the business $28,571.42. (This may be off as I'm not that
good at long division.) If you wanted to lower the threshold and
apply only his 10% figure, you would have to lower it to $2,857.14.

sections.question_synthetic: How should a filer compute the reporting threshold for income from a business entity when both the filer and their spouse own shares in that entity?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: When a filer and spouse both own shares in a business entity, the filer's attributable interest includes their pro rata share plus 50% of the spouse's pro rata share (due to community property rights), which determines whether income sources meet the $10,000 reportable threshold. The $10,000 threshold itself does not change, but the filer's effective ownership percentage increases for purposes of meeting that threshold.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Memorandum format without formal Q/A sections; question and conclusion extracted from memo content and subject line; extensive explanatory examples provided; author notes mathematical uncertainty in calculations

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should a filer compute the reporting threshold for income from a business entity when both the filer and their spouse own shares in that entity?

CONCLUSION: When a filer and spouse both own shares in a business entity, the filer's attributable interest includes their pro rata share plus 50% of the spouse's pro rata share (due to community property rights), which determines whether income sources meet the $10,000 reportable threshold. The $10,000 threshold itself does not change, but the filer's effective ownership percentage increases for purposes of meeting that threshold.

embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum explaining how to calculate reportable income thresholds from business entities when both the filer and spouse have ownership interests, clarifying that community property rights increase the filer's attributable share for threshold determination purposes.
